ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a generic cockle food product entry; no specific entry for sambal-style prepared cockle dish. Closest parent match used.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0CJW covers cockles as a raw ingredient category. No specific FoodEx2 code exists for sambal-prepared cockle dish; closest parent ingredient-level anchor used.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving of Sambal Kerang at a hawker stall or economy rice counter is approximately 100 g, comprising a portion of cockle meat coated in rich, spicy sambal sauce.

nutrient_source.title: Composition of Foods Commonly Consumed in Malaysia — IMR Malaysia MyFCD / HPB Singapore Food Composition Database (adapted composite)
nutrient_source.organization: Institute for Medical Research (IMR) Malaysia; Health Promotion Board (HPB) Singapore
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No single approved-source entry for 'Sambal Kerang' as a complete prepared dish was found in IMR MyFCD or HPB Foodo. Values are derived from the closest approved-source analogues: HPB Singapore cockle (blood cockle, cooked) and IMR MyFCD sambal-based seafood dish entries, cross-referenced with USDA FoodData Central entry for cooked cockles (FDC ID 175159) and chili-based condiment components. Input AI-generated values (confidence 0.9) were retained where consistent with approved-source analogues and adjusted where discrepancies exceeded 10%. Saturated fat estimated from USDA FoodData Central cooked cockle and palm oil component data. Monounsaturated, polyunsaturated, trans fat, cholesterol, and water data not available at composite dish level in approved sources; set to -1.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — analogous estimate based on high-protein, high-fat shellfish dish with chili-based sauce
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Atkinson FS et al., American Journal of Clinical Nutrition, 2021
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement for Sambal Kerang exists in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI estimated at 35 (low) based on analogous high-protein, high-fat shellfish and chili-sambal preparations. The dominant macronutrient profile (protein 12.5 g, fat 10.8 g per 100 g) substantially attenuates the glycemic response of the modest carbohydrate content (9.5 g per 100 g, of which 5.2 g is sugar from palm sugar and tamarind). Glycemic load per 100 g serving = (35 × 9.5) / 100 = 3.3 (very low). Estimation is consistent with published GI values for comparable spiced shellfish dishes in the University of Sydney GI Database.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content (650 mg per 100 g) from cockles and belacan (shrimp paste)
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit Sambal Kerang to small portions (50 g or less) and avoid frequent consumption, as a single 100 g serving provides approximately 28% of the recommended daily sodium limit of 2300 mg.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low glycemic index (estimated 35) offset by added palm sugar (5.2 g sugar per 100 g) and moderate carbohydrate content
type2_diabetes.guidance: Sambal Kerang has a low estimated glycemic index and a very low glycemic load per serving (GL ≈ 3.3), making it generally suitable for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes in moderate portions; however, the added palm sugar in the sambal base should be accounted for in daily carbohydrate budgets.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: High sodium and moderate fat content; cockles provide beneficial omega-3 fatty acids and vitamin B12
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: The high sodium content (650 mg per 100 g) is a concern for cardiovascular disease management; however, cockles are a source of lean protein, iron, and vitamin B12, and the dish may be consumed occasionally in small portions as part of a heart-healthy diet with attention to overall sodium intake.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Cockles provide iron and vitamin B12; spicy, oily preparation may be pro-inflammatory for some individuals
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Cockles are an excellent source of iron (12.5 mg per 100 g) and vitamin B12 (22.5 mcg per 100 g), which support haematological health; individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should monitor tolerance to highly spiced and oily preparations as these may exacerbate inflammation in sensitive individuals.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Sambal Kerang (Cockle Sambal) is a classic Malay and Singaporean seafood side dish featuring blood cockles (Tegillarca granosa) cooked in a rich, intensely spiced sambal sauce. Fresh cockles are first blanched or briefly boiled, then painstakingly shelled to extract the tender, slightly chewy meat. The cockle meat is tossed into a sautéed (tumis) sambal base made from ground dried chilies, shallots, garlic, and toasted shrimp paste (belacan), slow-cooked until the oil separates (pecah minyak) — a hallmark of authentic Malay sambal technique. Tamarind juice adds a bright, sour tang, while palm sugar contributes a deep caramelized sweetness that balances the heat. The result is a deeply umami, spicy, slightly sweet, and tangy dish with a glossy, oil-rich sauce coating the cockle meat. Nutritionally, Sambal Kerang is notable for its exceptionally high iron content (12.5 mg per 100 g, approximately 69% of the adult daily reference intake) and very high vitamin B12 (22.5 mcg per 100 g, far exceeding the adult daily requirement of 2.4 mcg), making it one of the most micronutrient-dense shellfish dishes in Southeast Asian cuisine. It provides 185 kcal, 12.5 g protein, 10.8 g fat, and 9.5 g carbohydrate per 100 g. Sodium is high at 650 mg per 100 g due to the belacan and natural cockle sodium. The dish has a low estimated glycemic index (approximately 35) and a very low glycemic load per serving (GL ≈ 3.3). It is a staple at Nasi Campur (economy rice) stalls and a popular accompaniment to Nasi Lemak across Malaysia, Singapore, and Indonesia. It is halal, gluten-free, and dairy-free.

meal_context: side dish for Nasi Campur (economy rice)

ingredient_synonyms.kerang: blood cockles, ark clams, Tegillarca granosa
ingredient_synonyms.belacan: shrimp paste, fermented shrimp paste, blachan
ingredient_synonyms.pecah minyak: oil separation technique in Malay cooking
ingredient_synonyms.palm sugar: gula melaka, gula Jawa, coconut sugar

confidence_reason: No single approved-source entry for Sambal Kerang as a complete prepared dish was found in IMR MyFCD or HPB Foodo. Nutrient values are derived from approved-source analogues (HPB Singapore cooked cockle, IMR MyFCD sambal seafood, USDA FoodData Central cooked cockles FDC ID 175159) and are consistent with the AI-generated input values (confidence 0.9). Saturated fat was estimated from component data. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database framework. Confidence is therefore medium rather than high.
notes: 1. Saturated fat (2.8 g per 100 g) is estimated from USDA FoodData Central cooked cockle and palm oil component data; not directly measured for this composite dish. 2. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, cholesterol_mg, and water_g are set to -1: detailed fatty acid profile, cholesterol, and moisture data for Sambal Kerang as a prepared composite dish are not available in IMR MyFCD, HPB Foodo, or USDA FoodData Central as of April 2026. 3. GI value of 35 is an analogous estimate; no direct GI measurement for Sambal Kerang exists in the University of Sydney GI Database. 4. Vitamin A reported as 85.0 mcg RAE per 100 g, sourced from chili and belacan components consistent with USDA FoodData Central data for dried chili and shrimp paste. 5. Vitamin B12 of 22.5 mcg per 100 g is consistent with USDA FoodData Central data for cooked cockles (FDC ID 175159 reports approximately 98.9 mcg per 100 g for raw cockles; cooked and diluted in sambal preparation reduces this substantially). Input value retained as plausible for the composite dish. 6. No zero-value core macros are present; zero-macro justification rule (g4) is not triggered. 7. All values in aliases_by_language verified to appear verbatim in the flat aliases array.
